episodeOutline.title: The Doodle Kingdom
episodeOutline.synopsis: A joke that costs more than Wren realizes in the moment kicks off a week of forced proximity, awkward repair attempts, and one very deliberate seating chart courtesy of Mr. Alvarez. When Wren is paired with Alex — the exact person she just publicly embarrassed — she has to figure out whether an apology is a thing you say once or a thing you keep proving, especially when the easiest laugh in the room is still sitting right there, waiting to be taken again.

scenes.title: The Easy Laugh
scenes.description: Wren's found her groove at Maya's table — the deflecting joke, the perfectly timed line. When Maya fishes for something funny, Wren reaches for the easiest material in her pocket: a riff on the elaborate invented kingdom Alex once let her glimpse in the margins of a notebook, in confidence. The table erupts. Twelve feet away, at the end of another table, Alex goes very still, then packs up without a word and walks out.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The cost of the performance becomes visible for the first time — a real person, not a social maneuver.

scenes.title: The Drop
scenes.description: The laugh is still hanging in the air. Jordan's smile falters half a second too late. Maya's already moved on like nothing happened. Wren's stomach drops as the cafeteria door swings shut behind Alex.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The gap between performance and feeling cracks open.

scenes.title: One Shot
scenes.description: Wren catches Alex by the lockers. Alex won't quite look at her, arms crossed around the notebook like a shield. Whatever Wren says next either lands as an actual apology or as more noise.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The first real test of honesty over cleverness.

scenes.title: The Narrow Window
scenes.description: Wren tells herself she'll catch Alex after class. The bell rings before she fully decides. She spots Alex slipping into Mr. Alvarez's room ahead of her, spine very straight — a narrow window in the crowd, right before the door closes.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Guilt curdling into avoidance, with one last chance to choose differently.

scenes.title: Named Together
scenes.description: Mr. Alvarez announces the 'Identity Map' project — pairs, not chosen by students. He reads the list like he already knew exactly who needed to be put together: 'Wren and Alex.' Alex's shoulders don't fully unclench, but they don't fully close either.

scenes.title: Named Together, Anyway
scenes.description: Same announcement, same pairing — but Alex's face closes like a door the second the names are read together. Whatever chance existed didn't get taken, and now they're stuck together anyway.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Forced proximity with an unhealed wound.

scenes.title: The Real Map
scenes.description: Alex actually starts talking — cautiously showing Wren the real page of the invented world, not the punchline version. Then, quietly, sets a rule: 'If we're doing this, you don't get to use my stuff as material. Not ever. Not even the safe version.' It's said like a boundary that's been broken before.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Trust extended in exchange for a boundary named out loud.

scenes.title: Minimum Required
scenes.description: Alex keeps the project strictly professional — hands over the minimum, nothing more. When Wren pushes for more material, Alex finally snaps, quiet and precise: 'Why should I trust you with anything else?' It's a real question, not rhetorical.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A closed door with one small crack of an opening.

scenes.title: The Line
scenes.description: Right before Wren and Alex present, Maya leans over with a smirk: 'Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom.' The old easy laugh is right there, free for the taking — and Alex is close enough to hear.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The choice that proves whether the apology was real.

scenes.title: The Line, Thinner Ice
scenes.description: Same moment, higher stakes — the trust between Wren and Alex is thinner this time. Maya's comment lands right as Alex double-checks the poster board, a visible flinch, waiting to see which Wren shows up.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A second chance to prove the first apology wasn't the only real thing she'll say.

scenes.title: The Line, Already Crossed Once
scenes.description: Alex has barely spoken to Wren all week. When Maya's comment lands, Alex doesn't wait to see what Wren does — already stepping back, already expecting the worst, notebook held tight like the first time.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The relationship is on the edge of over before it started.

scenes.title: Same Time Tomorrow
scenes.description: Alex smiles — small, real, surprised by itself. Doesn't say the friendship is fixed. Says: 'Same time tomorrow? I want to show you the part I didn't present.' Not everything. Enough to build on.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Earned, fragile hope — repair as ongoing practice, not a fixed event.

scenes.title: The Door, Cracked
scenes.description: Alex doesn't say much, but doesn't walk away either — just a nod, a 'see you tomorrow,' the door left open a crack instead of shut. Whatever gets built from here is still Wren's to earn.

scenes.title: The Empty Seat
scenes.description: Maya's table is warmer to Wren than ever — she 'gets it' now. But there's an empty seat two tables over where Alex sits alone, and Wren can't stop noticing it, every day after.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging that costs more than it gives — the hollow win.

scenes.title: No Eye Contact
scenes.description: Alex has stopped making eye contact in the hallway entirely. The project got finished, technically, in near-silence. Whatever might have been possible is quietly, permanently closed.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The real cost of choices left unaddressed.

choicePoints.prompt: Go after Alex, or stay at the table?
choicePoints.context: Getting up now means leaving Maya mid-sentence, in front of everyone.

options.text: Get up and go find Alex
Stay, laugh along, fix it later

choicePoints.context: Alex is waiting, guarded, giving you exactly one shot before the bell.

options.text: Name exactly what you did and why it was wrong — no excuses
Apologize, but explain you were just trying to be funny

choicePoints.prompt: Take the last chance in the hallway, or let the bell decide?
choicePoints.context: It would mean pushing through the crowd to catch Alex in the ten seconds before class starts.

options.text: Push through and say something, even rushed
Let it go — deal with it later

choicePoints.prompt: How do you respond to the boundary Alex just set?
choicePoints.context: Alex is watching to see if the apology was real or just a performance of one.

options.text: I hear you. That's fair — thank you for telling me.
I already said sorry, what more do you want from me?

choicePoints.context: This is the first real chance to explain the why behind the joke — not excuse it, explain it.

options.text: Admit the truth: you wanted to matter at that table, and it cost you something you didn't mean to spend
Deflect: 'It's not that big a deal, you're overreacting'

choicePoints.prompt: Take the easy laugh, or hold the line?

options.text: Shut it down and mean it: 'It's actually really good — you'll see.'
Laugh it off vaguely to keep the peace with Maya's table

choicePoints.prompt: Take the easy laugh, or hold the line?
choicePoints.context: The stakes are higher this time because the trust between you is thinner.

options.text: Hold the line, clearly and publicly

choicePoints.prompt: One last real chance, or the pattern repeats?
choicePoints.context: Alex has stopped waiting for you to be different.

options.text: Stop everything and actually mean it this time — apologize again, in front of everyone if that's what it takes
Freeze, say nothing, let the moment pass

branches.name: The Authentic Repair Path
branches.description: Wren chooses honesty and accountability at every turn — chasing Alex down immediately, naming the harm specifically, accepting the boundary without defensiveness, and holding the line publicly when it costs her socially.
branches.outcome: A real, if fragile, friendship begins — Alex extends an invitation to see more of the invented world, on their own terms.

branches.name: The Late Repair Path
branches.description: Wren stumbles at the first opportunity but recovers — catching a narrow second chance, answering honestly under pressure, and eventually holding the line when it matters.
branches.outcome: Trust is rebuilt slowly and incompletely; the relationship ends the episode uncertain but open.

branches.name: The Performed Apology Path
branches.description: Wren says the right words early but never actually changes — deflecting blame, resenting the boundary, and ultimately choosing Maya's table over Alex when it counts.
branches.outcome: Wren gains social ease at the popular table but loses the one relationship that might have mattered, and knows it.

branches.name: The Avoidance Path
branches.description: Wren avoids every opportunity for honesty, letting silence and self-protection compound the original harm until Alex stops expecting anything different.
branches.outcome: The relationship closes, quietly and permanently, and Wren has to sit with what that costs.

emotionalArc: Comfortable performance and belonging-by-proximity at Maya's table
Sudden, visceral guilt as the human cost of the joke becomes visible
Dissonance between wanting to fix it and wanting to protect the social win
Forced proximity via the project removes the option to avoid the problem
A boundary is named out loud, testing whether the apology was real
Public pressure re-tempts Wren with the original harm, now with full knowledge of its cost
Resolution lands somewhere on a spectrum from earned trust to quiet, permanent loss

characterArcs.arc: Moves from treating social ease as the goal to discovering that the performance that gets her a seat at any table can cost her the one relationship built on being actually known.
Tests, cautiously and specifically, whether this new place will repeat the pattern of being someone else's material — and whether an apology this time means anything under pressure.

Engineers the forced pairing deliberately, betting on discomfort now over isolation later — his own quiet history with being the outsider never stated, only implied in how carefully he watches the two of them work.
Remains, unchanged, the gravitational pull toward the version of belonging that costs nothing and means nothing — a mirror for what Wren's instincts could calcify into.
Registers the discomfort of the initial joke but never intervenes, modeling the limits of conflict-avoidant warmth and making room for Wren's own growth to matter more.

traitMapping.description: Tracked through how specifically and directly Wren names the harm, states her reasoning, and defends Alex out loud versus deflecting, hedging, or staying silent under social pressure.
Tracked through whether Wren notices and acts on Alex's visible hurt in real time, and whether she can name her own underlying motive (wanting to belong) honestly rather than minimizing it.
Tracked through whether Wren's apology is followed by consistent behavior — accepting the boundary, not repeating the harm, and holding her ground publicly even when it costs social capital.

relationshipDynamics.dynamic: The emotional core of the episode — a specific, guarded kid testing whether this new place repeats an old pattern, and whether Wren's noticing translates into consistent action.
A referendum on whether Wren mistakes magnetism for belonging; Maya never changes, so every return to her table is a choice, not a default.
An adult who engineers discomfort on purpose, believing it prevents isolation later — his intervention creates the structure but never does the emotional work for Wren.
A low-stakes, conflict-avoidant presence whose failure to intervene highlights exactly what Wren has to do that easier friendships don't require.

replayHooks: The apology scene (choice-2 / choice-2b) plays out completely differently depending on timing and specificity, changing Alex's guardedness for the rest of the episode.
Only the fully authentic path unlocks Alex actually sharing the real invented-world map rather than the guarded minimum.
The climax choice (choice-4/4b/4c) can be replayed to see the same public moment resolve as triumph, hollow victory, or quiet loss depending on everything that came before.
Players who take the 'popularity cost' path get a distinct epilogue that reframes the whole episode as a warning rather than a triumph on replay.
Mr. Alvarez's brief reactions shift subtly across branches, rewarding attentive replays with hints about why he engineered the pairing at all.
educationalGoals: Model the difference between saying 'sorry' and actually repairing harm through specific acknowledgment and changed behavior
Show boundary-setting as a normal, healthy act rather than a conflict to be avoided
Demonstrate that integrity is tested most under peer pressure, not in private
Illustrate emotional awareness as noticing impact on others in real time, not just intent
conversationStarters: Was there a moment where you would have made a different choice than Wren did? What would you have said instead?
Why do you think Alex's boundary in the library mattered so much, even after Wren already apologized?
What do you think Mr. Alvarez was hoping would happen when he paired them up on purpose?
Which ending felt most true to how real friendships actually get repaired — or not repaired?
Have you ever had to choose between an easy laugh and defending someone? What did you do?

designNotes: The structural bet here is that the 'educational content' (repair, apology, boundaries) is entirely carried by specific, escalating dramatic pressure rather than any explicit lesson-language. The cold open commits the harm without player agency so every subsequent choice is genuinely about repair, not about whether to cause harm in the first place — this keeps the episode's moral center consistent across replays while still allowing real variance in outcome quality. Mr. Alvarez's forced pairing is treated as a plot-mandatory beat (not optional) to reflect his character brief as an active architect of pressure, which also elegantly reconverges the two early branches into a shared structural spine without erasing their emotional differences. The four-ending spread (friendship / tentative / lonely-win / broken) is designed so that 'success' isn't binary — the lonely-win ending in particular is meant to be the most quietly unsettling, since it looks like a win on the surface (Wren gets the popular table) while the episode frames it as the actual loss.
uncertainties: The lonely-win ending is emotionally the most sophisticated but risks reading as 'punishing' the player for a plausible, realistic choice (choosing peace with Maya over confrontation) — playtesting with actual target-age kids would help confirm the tone lands as bittersweet rather than preachy.

Convergence of two early branches (chase vs. delay) into shared pairing scenes is efficient but slightly reduces the feeling of divergence in the middle of the episode; an alternative would fully separate the two tracks at the cost of roughly 40% more scenes.

Three target traits map cleanly, but EMOTIONAL_AWARENESS and COMMUNICATION co-occur in almost every choice — a future revision might want at least one choice point that isolates COMMUNICATION from EMOTIONAL_AWARENESS more sharply to get cleaner signal.
alternativesConsidered: Making the inciting joke itself a player choice (i.e., deciding whether to make fun of Alex at all) — rejected because it would let cautious players skip the entire repair mechanic, undermining the episode's core theme.

A fully branching structure with no reconvergence at the pairing scene, giving four entirely separate scene chains — considered for maximum divergence but rejected as exceeding the 10-15 minute runtime target without proportional narrative payoff.

Adding a scene where Jordan actively intervenes to defend Alex, giving the player a model to react to — set aside to preserve Jordan's established conflict-avoidant trait and keep the moral weight squarely on the player's own choices.
